---
title: "Austie and Lemass"
---

Lemass was a great man, you see, a man of conviction, of ideals, unlike the fellas that came before him. The problem with the pro-treaty crowd, he always maintained, was that they had no convictions. They were men who were happy to settle for second best and while that brought peace it couldn't bring prosperity because planning for prosperity requires prioritization. And if you have no convictions, you can't prioritize one group over another because you are too soft to upset the crowd that loses out. During the thirties, it was us industry men who were the priority and the cattle men lost out. And Lemass could live with that because his convictions told him so.

But you see he was also a man who could organize other men, and that was why he was good for the country. He knew from the brigades how to pull men together to stop the squabbling between them. He told me a good one about when he first started in the department and he wanted to meet all the fellas working for him but they wouldn't meet him together. He asked them what was going on and they told him how it was. This fella doesn't talk to so and so, and these two won't sit in the one room together. And so on and so forth. So he brought in a fella called Leydon to sort it out and soon enough the whole place was ticking. And if he didn't have their cooperation, I'd say they knew all about it because Lemass had no trouble letting fellas know who was boss. Before you knew it the department had to bring in a heap of extra fella to do all the extra work that was being created with the new industries. .

You see, The department was lashing out grants left right and centre in the thirties
